title: Psalms 13
subtitle: How Long, LORD?

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “How Long Will You Forget Me?”

heb.paragraph: "How long (ʿad-ʾānâ), LORD? Will you forget me forever?" (v.1). Four times "how long" in two verses — the most compressed lament in the Psalter. The repetition is desperation: how long forgotten, how long hidden, how long sorrow, how long enemy triumph? The psalm is only six verses but contains the entire lament arc: complaint, petition, trust.

refs.note: "How long, LORD? Will you hide yourself forever?" — the same cry at a national level.
"How long, Sovereign Lord?" — the martyrs’ cry echoes David’s.

notes.note: Four "how long" questions hammer the same point: the wait feels eternal. God seems to have forgotten. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the four questions create a rhythmic assault on God’s silence. The repetition IS the argument: this has gone on too long. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: the question "will you forget me forever?" is prayer, not accusation. Calvin: the one who asks God "have you forgotten?" still believes God can remember. It is faith expressed as complaint. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ʿad-ʾānâ (until when?) appears four times in vv.1–2, creating an anaphoric structure that builds urgency with each repetition. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "forever?" is not theology but emotion. David knows God does not forget (Isa 49:15). But suffering FEELS like forever. The psalm legitimises the feeling. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: this is the model lament: brief, honest, structurally complete. The four "how long" questions, the petitions, and the trust-pivot (vv.5–6) in just six verses. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the fourfold "how long" is not patience but urgency. Each repetition escalates: forgotten → hidden face → daily wrestling → enemy triumph. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: The shortest complete lament. David asks four "how long" questions (vv.1–2), makes two petitions (vv.3–4: look at me, answer me, give light to my eyes lest I sleep in death, lest my enemy triumph). Six verses, complete emotional arc.

sections.header: Verses 5–6 — “I Trust in Your Unfailing Love”

heb.gloss: unfailing love / steadfast love / loyal love
heb.paragraph: "But I trust in your unfailing love (ḥesed); my heart rejoices in your salvation" (v.5). The pivot: from "how long" to "but I trust." Two verses of trust balance four questions of despair. Ḥesed is God’s covenant loyalty — love that endures when feelings fail.

refs.note: "I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" — the same ḥesed-trust declaration.
"Because of the LORD’s great love we are not consumed" — the same theology in deeper darkness.

notes.note: "But I trust in your unfailing love." The single most important word in lament psalms: "but." Everything before it is complaint; everything after it is faith.
"I will sing the LORD’s praise, for he has been good to me." The psalm ends in song. From tears to music in six verses.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the compression is remarkable: two verses of confidence after four of despair. The disproportion is the point — a little trust outweighs much suffering. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "but I trust" is the heart of the Christian life. Calvin: the "but" is not denial of pain but refusal to let pain have the last word. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥesed (covenant love/loyalty) is the Psalter’s most important theological term. It describes God’s self-imposed obligation to love his covenant people. David trusts this, not his feelings. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the "but" of v.5 is the hinge of every lament. Without it, the psalm is despair. With it, despair becomes trust. The turn is a choice, not a feeling. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the shift from lament to praise happens without any change in David’s circumstances. Nothing external changed. The decision to trust changed everything internal. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: ḥesed-trust is the bedrock. When feelings say "forgotten," covenant love says "held." The psalm chooses covenant over experience. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: The pivot is the word "but" (v.5). After four questions and two petitions, David declares trust: "but I trust in your ḥesed." His heart rejoices in God’s salvation. He will sing because God has been good (v.6). The psalm moves from darkness to song in one verse. The shift is not based on changed circumstances but on chosen trust.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The stakes: without God’s response, David dies…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVand my enemy will say, “I have overcome him,” and my foes will rejoice when I fall.ESVlest my enemy say, \u201cI have prevailed over him,\u201d lest my foes rejoice because I am shaken.NIVBut I trust in your unfailing love; my heart rejoices in your salvation.ESVBut I have trusted in your steadfast love; my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.

rec.text: "Four "how long" questions hammer the same point: the wait feels eternal. God seems to have forgotten."

rows.label: The Cry — 13:1–4
rows.text: Four "how long?" questions. The compressed lament. "Give light to my eyes or I sleep in death."

hebtext.note: "How long (ʿad-ʾānâ), LORD? Will you forget me forever?" (v.1). Four times "how long" in two verses — the most compressed lament in the Psalter. The repetition is desperation: how long forgotten, how long hidden, how long sorrow, how long enemy triumph? The psalm is only six verses but contains the entire lament arc: complaint, petition, trust.

hebtext.gloss: unfailing love / steadfast love / loyal love
hebtext.note: "But I trust in your unfailing love (ḥesed); my heart rejoices in your salvation" (v.5). The pivot: from "how long" to "but I trust." Two verses of trust balance four questions of despair. Ḥesed is God’s covenant loyalty — love that endures when feelings fail.

thread.text: "How long, LORD? Will you hide yourself forever?" — the same cry at a national level.
"How long, Sovereign Lord?" — the martyrs’ cry echoes David’s.
"I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" — the same ḥesed-trust declaration.
"Because of the LORD’s great love we are not consumed" — the same theology in deeper darkness.

themes.note: Faith peaks: the "but I trust" pivot is the essence of lament faith. Mercy: ḥesed is the ground of trust. The perfect six-verse model of complaint → trust.

prayer_prompt: As you consider the fourfold 'How long?' of this psalm, consider asking God whether you can sustain honest complaint without letting it curdle into despair — and whether trust can coexist with unanswered questions.
